input.title: Understanding Central Tendency in Enrollment Data
input.setup_prose: As a Registrar's Office analyst, you are preparing a report on course enrollment sizes. You notice a few very large lecture courses and many smaller seminars, creating a skewed distribution. You decide to look at the 'median' rather than the 'mean' to summarize the typical student experience.
worked_steps: Step 1: Visualize the data — create a histogram to see the spread of enrollment sizes across all courses.
Step 2: Identify the skew — observe that most courses have fewer than 50 students, but a few large lectures pull the mean upward.
Step 3: Select the appropriate measure — choose the median as the best indicator of 'central-tendency' because it reflects the middle course size, unaffected by outliers.
input.interpretation: In this scenario, the Registrar uses the 'median' to represent the typical course enrollment. Unlike the mean, the median is not heavily influenced by a single large introductory lecture, allowing the Registrar to make decisions based on the enrollment level experienced by the majority of students. This is a common strategy for interpreting 'central-tendency' in skewed data.

chart.caption: Distribution of undergraduate course enrollments (Fall 2023). Most courses are small, with a long tail of very large lectures.

input.title: Summarizing Applicant Experience
input.setup_prose: You are reviewing the professional experience of 82 applicants for a professional master's program. To help the admissions committee understand the cohort's background, you need to summarize their 'typical' years of experience.
input.artifact_summary_stats: Median: 4 years; Mean: 4.8 years; Range: 0-12 years.
input.observation_prompt: Before answering, take a moment: what do you notice about the distribution of experience years in this chart?
input.prompt: As an admissions officer, you are summarizing the professional experience of your incoming class for a report. Based on the data above, which value best describes the 'typical' experience of an applicant, and why?

options.text: The mean (4.8 years) is the best representation of the typical applicant's experience.
options.feedback: You might choose this because the mean is a common way to describe an 'average,' but the presence of a few applicants with 10-12 years of experience pulls the mean upward, making it less representative of the typical applicant.

options.text: The median (4 years) is the best representation of the typical applicant's experience.
options.feedback: Choosing the median is appropriate here because it represents the middle value, which is less affected by the few applicants with high experience who pull the mean to the right. It tells us that half the cohort has 4 or fewer years of experience.

options.text: The range (0 to 12 years) is the best way to understand the central tendency of the cohort.
options.feedback: This is a common reaction when looking at the full spread of data. However, the range only tells us the distance between the extremes and does not identify the most common or 'typical' central point within the cohort.

options.text: Saying 'most applicants have between 2 and 6 years' is more accurate than choosing a single central tendency value.
options.feedback: You might consider this because the chart shows a concentration of applicants between 2 and 6 years. However, 'most' is subjective, whereas the median provides a precise, calculated central point that splits the group in two.

input.hint: Observe the peak of the histogram and consider where the 'middle' data point (median) sits compared to the 'average' (mean) when the distribution leans toward higher experience values.

chart.caption: Distribution of years of professional experience among the 2024 cohort of applicants for a specific professional master's program.

viz_decision.reason: rule-5: previous turn already used chart → vary or omit

input.title: Evaluating Donor Distributions
input.setup_prose: You are working in the Office of Alumni Affairs and reviewing a campaign summary. You notice a significant disparity between the average gift and the middle-value gift, which is complicating your planning for the next outreach cycle.
input.prompt: Looking at this report, you see a mean gift amount of $5,000 and a median gift amount of $500 for a recent fundraising campaign. How would you interpret these two values in the context of donor strategy?
consider_scaffolds: What do you conclude about the typical gift amount based on the median compared to the mean?
Which specific aspects of the data lead you to this conclusion?
What follow-up question would you ask about the donor pool to better understand this distribution?
input.rubric_primary_criterion: The response should successfully identify that the gap between mean and median suggests a skewed distribution with potential outliers, rather than focusing solely on the calculation.

dictionary_handoff.handoff_question: Is this how your school uses this term?

dictionary_handoff.handoffQuestion: Is this how your school uses this term?

viz_decision.reason: rule-3: constructive tier → learner constructs interpretation, no visual crutch

input.title: Choosing a Representative Measure
input.setup_prose: You are reviewing a report on the time it takes students in your graduate program to complete their thesis. The data shows most students finish in 3–4 years, but a small group of doctoral candidates took 8+ years due to research delays. Your department head asks for a single number to report as the 'typical' experience.
input.prompt: Given the data distribution, which measure of central tendency—the mean or the median—would you recommend to the department head to represent the 'typical' time to completion for this cohort, and why?
input.rubric_primary_criterion: Evaluation of the logic connecting the choice of measure to the specific characteristics of the distribution (e.g., sensitivity to outliers).

dictionary_handoff.handoff_question: These terms came up: central-tendency, median. Which of these did your reasoning depend on, and how does your team use it?

dictionary_handoff.handoffQuestion: These terms came up: central-tendency, median. Which of these did your reasoning depend on, and how does your team use it?

viz_decision.reason: rule-2: WikiDraft tier → contribution-routine flowchart (process, not content)

input.title: Contributing to the Harvard Data Dictionary
input.framing_prose: You have consistently demonstrated a nuanced understanding of how summary metrics like central-tendency and median shape institutional strategy. We are now inviting you to help build our cross-school knowledge base by drafting a formal entry for our shared terminology guide. Your contribution will be reviewed by peers and data governance leads before being considered for publication.
input.draft_template_hint: Focus on how your department distinguishes between mean and median for resource allocation decisions.

flowchart.caption: The dictionary contribution loop for Harvard staff.

draftTemplate.howWeUseItPlaceholder: How does your team use this term?
draftTemplate.examplePlaceholder: A short example from your work.
draftTemplate.differsPlaceholder: How does this differ from how other schools use it? (optional)
